coredata.dc:title: Facile wound dressing replacement: Carbon dots for dissolving alginate hydrogels via competitive complexation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qian
Shen, Xin
Liu, Chenguang
Xing, Dongming
coredata.dc:description: Dissolvable hydrogels have shown great potentials in removing the exudes and alleviating pain during wound dressing replacement. Herein, a series of carbon dots (CDs) composing high complexation ability with Cu2+ were prepared for capturing Cu2+ from Cu2+-alginate hydrogels. CDs were prepared using biocompatible lysine as the main starting material, while ethylenediamine was chosen as the other starting material for its extremely high complexation ability with Cu2+. The complexation ability increased with the increasing amount of ethylenediamine, while the cell viability decreased. When the mass ratio of ethylenediamine to lysine in CDs was above 1/4, six-coordinate copper centers could be formed. CD1/4 at the concentration of 90 mg/mL could dissolve Cu2+-alginate hydrogels within 16 min, which was about twice faster than lysine. In vivo results showed the replaced hydrogels could ameliorate hypoxic conditions, alleviate local inflammatory reactions and promote the healing speed of burn wounds. Thus, the above results suggested the competitive complexation of CDs with Cu2+ could effectively dissolve Cu2+-alginate hydrogels, which have much potential in realizing facile wound dressing replacement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Alginate
Dissolvable hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of metal ion-alginate hydrogels
In vivo burn wound healing test
Dissolution properties of CDs on different metal iron-alginate hydrogels
Complexation abilities of CDs toward Cu2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2020MH324 and ZR2020QH271 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs (A) TEM image of CD1/4, (B) size distribution of CD1/4, (C) Optical properties of CD1/4, CD0 and lysine, (D) FTIR spectra of CDs and lysine, (E) Raman spectra of CDs and lysine.

XPS and high-resolution spectra of CDs and lysine (A) XPS spectrum of CD1/3, (B) XPS spectrum of CD0, (C) XPS spectrum of lysine, (D) C 1s spectra of CD1/3, (E) C 1s spectra of CD0, (F) C 1s spectra of lysine.

Interaction between CD1/4 and metal ions or metal ion-alginate hydrogels (A) metal ion-alginate hydrogels before dissolution (the concentration of each metal ion was 0.1 M), (B) metal ion-alginate hydrogels after dissolution, (C) residual hydrogels after dissolution, (D) PL intensity of CD1/4 after adding different metal ions (75 μM).

Colorimetric responses of the complexation between CDs and Cu2+ (A) color reactions between CDs and Cu2+; (B) the effect of different types of CDs on light-absorption; the effect of Cu2+ concentration on light-absorption of (C) Cu2+-CD0 complexes, (D) Cu2+-CD1/4 complexes, (E) Cu2+-lysine complexes, (F) Cu2+-EDA complexes; (G) schematic representation of Cu2+-CDs complexation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Morphology and dissolving of Cu2+-alginate hydrogels. (A) SEM of Cu2+-alginate hydrogel, (B) dissolving of hydrogels after treating with 90 mg/mL dissolving agents for 16 min, (C) dissolving of hydrogels at different time points, (D) FTIR of sodium alginate (orange line), Cu2+-alginate hydrogel (blue line) and Cu2+-alginate hydrogel after dissolving and dialysis (yellow line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Evaluation of the in vivo therapeutic effects. (A) Photographs during treatments, (B) unclosed wound rates for various treatments; (C) H&E, Masson and IHC staining of skin tissue sections in 19 days after wounding, (D) collagen volume fraction, (E) the HIF-1α-positive cell density, (F) average optical density (AOD) analysis for CD 68 of skin tissues after various treatments. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Facile wound dressing replacement: Carbon dots for dissolving alginate hydrogels via competitive complexation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Institute, the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cancer Institute, the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Marine Life Science, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266003, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Marine Life Science, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: D. Xing, Cancer Institute, the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dissolvable hydrogels have shown great potentials in removing the exudes and alleviating pain during wound dressing replacement. Herein, a series of carbon dots (CDs) composing high complexation ability with Cu2+ were prepared for capturing Cu2+ from Cu2+-alginate hydrogels. CDs were prepared using biocompatible lysine as the main starting material, while ethylenediamine was chosen as the other starting material for its extremely high complexation ability with Cu2+. The complexation ability increased with the increasing amount of ethylenediamine, while the cell viability decreased. When the mass ratio of ethylenediamine to lysine in CDs was above 1/4, six-coordinate copper centers could be formed. CD1/4 at the concentration of 90 mg/mL could dissolve Cu2+-alginate hydrogels within 16 min, which was about twice faster than lysine. In vivo results showed the replaced hydrogels could ameliorate hypoxic conditions, alleviate local inflammatory reactions and promote the healing speed of burn wounds. Thus, the above results suggested the competitive complexation of CDs with Cu2+ could effectively dissolve Cu2+-alginate hydrogels, which have much potential in realizing facile wound dressing replacement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound healing is an evolving process that takes place over days to months depending on severity, and wound dressings need to be replaced to remove the exudes from the wounds [1,2]. These dressing replacements not only take significant time (57 ± 34 min), but also require mechanical debridement that could damage new tissues and cause pains [1,3]. In order to address the unmet need for facile wound dressing replacement, the concept of dissolvable hydrogel dressing has been introduced recently. Hydrogels are three-dimensional hydrophilic networks which could provide barrier against external pathogens and promote tissue infiltration [4]. To obtain dissolvable hydrogel dressing, specific stimulus such as amino acid solutions [5] and reducing agents [2] have been considered as feasible methods. However, chemical reactions for preparing these functional hydrogels are complex and involve several steps. Thus, it is still highly desired to construct faster, safer and more convenient approach for preparing dissolvable hydrogels on the wounds.

Alginate is a natural linear polysaccharide, consisting of repeating units of 1,4-β-D-mannuronic acid (M) and R-L-guluronic acid (G) residues [6]. Sodium alginate, as the sodium form of alginate with good water-solubility, has been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for many years [7]. It is one of the most significant biopolymers for production of biomaterials such as hydrogels, films, foams, wafers, nanofibers and gauzes [8–11]. These biomaterials could facilitate fast wound healing through hemostasis, absorbing exudates and creating moist environments [12,13]. Particularly, sodium alginate-based hydrogels have aroused much attractive in the fields of the medical and pharmaceutical industries for their in-situ, fast and easy formation. Sodium alginate can form three-dimensional hydrogels with the help of divalent cations (except for Mg2+), and possess egg-box structures in which two facing helical stretches bind the divalent ion via complexation [14]. However, it is still a big challenge to dissolve alginate hydrogels for wound dressing replacement.

Among various types of divalent cations, copper (II) ion (Cu2+) has been considered as a preferred crosslinker for preparing alginate hydrogels. Copper (Cu), as an indispensable trace metal in human body, provides beneficial effects on skin regeneration, cartilage and angiogenesis formation [15]. Cu2+ also possesses good antibacterial activity for its abilities to penetrate through the bacterial membrane, inhibit cell respiration and degrade DNA [16,17]. Considering its physiological and antibacterial activities, Cu2+ is an appropriate crosslinker for preparing hydrogels [16–18]. Hydrogels could be formed through complexing action between Cu2+ and carboxylate groups in alginate molecules, but this binding is non-selectivity [19]. This offers an opportunity for destroying Cu2+-alginate hydrogels via another stronger complexation.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention for their excellent complexing ability with Cu2+, good biocompatibility, ultra-small size and easy preparation [20]. Various works have reported functional groups on the surface of CDs possess high complexing ability [21]. These CDs have been widely used in chemical sensing area, however, their usage in wound healing is still very few. In this work, we prepared a series of CDs using lysine and ethylenediamine as the starting materials for dissolving Cu2+-alginate hydrogels covered on burn wounds. Compared to lysine, the formation of CDs increased the complexation ability and dissolving rate of hydrogels. Compared to ethylenediamine, CDs reduced the cytotoxicity. The in vivo results showed the healing speed of hydrogels with replacement was faster than that of the hydrogels without replacement and control groups, which opens a novel pathway to dissolve hydrogels and eliminate pain during wound dressing changes.

Lysine, ethylenediamine, sodium alginate (200 MPa·s), CaCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2, ZnSO4, FeSO4, trypsin and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were obtained from Gibco. Deionized water was used in all the experiments.

A simple one-pot hydrothermal method was used to prepare CDs. Lysine 0.8 g and ethylenediamine (0–0.26 g) were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water, and the samples were named according to mass ratio of ethylenediamine to lysine (Table S1). The mixed solution was transferred into 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and then heated at 180 °C for 7 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the resulting light-yellow solution was dialyzed against distilled water in a dialysis bag (MWCO 100–500) for 24 h. Finally, the CDs were vacuum freeze-dried and vacuum dried at 50 °C to obtain light yellow solids.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs were obtained using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900, Shimadzu Co., Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu Co., Japan), respectively. The morphology and size of CDs were measured through transmission electron microscopy (Beckman Coulter Inc. USA). The size distribution of the nanoparticles was obtained by manually measuring 300 particles using ImageJ (National Institute of Health, Maryland, USA). The groups on CDs were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Co., Czech), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrometer (Nicolet Co., USA) and Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Ltd., Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of metal ion-alginate hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of sodium alginate in water (14 mg/mL) was used to prepare hydrogels. After the sodium alginate was added into little bottle or wound, metal ions (Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ or Fe2+) at concentration of 0.1 mol/L were sprayed until a gel is formed. The morphology and microstructure of the hydrogels were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Co., Japan). The rheological properties were examined by measuring their storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) using a rheometer (Malvern Co., UK) equipped with a 20 mm parallel-plate geometry with a gap of 1 mm. Oscillatory frequency sweeps ranging from 0.1 to 10 Hz were conducted by applying a 1 % strain at room temperature [22].

CDs were used to trigger the dissolution of metal ion-alginate hydrogels. A volume of 0.3 mL CDs aqueous solution (0–110 mg/mL) was added to 0.6 g hydrogels, the mixture was kept at room temperature with gentle shaking. The dissolution of hydrogel was confirmed by turning the vial upside down and UV–vis absorption measurement. In order to further investigate the dissolution behavior of CDs on metal ion-alginate hydrogels, the dissolved hydrogel was investigated by FTIR measurement after dialysis against NaCl solution at concentration of 0.3 M. High concentration of NaCl, with the ability to rise the concentration of counter-ions [23], was added to break the electrostatic interactions between CDs and sodium alginate.

Cytotoxicity was investigated through MTT viability assay on L929 mouse fibroblast cells. Cells were seeded and cultured on 96-well plate in DMEM culture medium supplemented with 10 % FBS at 37 °C. In order to measure the cytotoxicity of CDs, the CDs solutions were sterilized by filtration (0.22 μm filter, Millipore). Then, cells were incubated with CDs (10 mg/mL) for 6 h and fresh cell culture medium for 12 h. To measure the cytotoxicity of Cu2+-alginate hydrogel, 100 μL of the liquid extraction medium was incubated with cells for 24 h. The hydrogel extraction solution was prepared by incubating freeze dried hydrogel (1–100 mg) in 10 mL DMEM media for 48 h at 37 °C. After the extraction process, the supernatant was sterilized by filtration [24]. In order to measure the cytotoxicity of hydrogel after dissolution, the dissolved hydrogels were lyophilized, redissolved, filtered and incubated with cells for 24 h. After cells were treated with MTT, and the absorbance values at 590 nm were measured using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo burn wound healing test
ce:section.ce:para: Animals were treated in accordance with protocols evaluated and approved by the Ethics Committee of Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. Six-week-old Sprague–Dawley male rats were used for experiments. After anesthetizing with pentobarbital sodium, the rats were depilated on the back. A burn wound with diameter 1.5 cm was made by preheated soldering iron at the temperature of 100 °C for 15 s at the dorsal area [5]. Twelve rats were randomly divided into three groups: (1) control group without any treatment, (2) the Cu2+-alginate hydrogel without replacement, (3) the Cu2+-alginate hydrogel replaced by CD1/4. For group (3), the hydrogel was surgically debrided from the wounds every four days until wound healing. Specifically, CD1/4 solution was sprayed to the surface of hydrogels for dissolution. The residents on wounds were gently washed by sterilized saline and cleaned by gauze, and then the wound was filled with sodium alginate and sprayed with Cu2+ solution again to form new hydrogels.

The wound tissues after 19 days treatment were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde overnight, embedded in paraffin and sectioned at 5 μm thickness. The sections were then stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining kit and Masson staining kit according to the manufacturer description. For the immunohistochemical (IHC) analysis, the slices were incubated with the CD68/HIF-1α monoclonal antibody and horseradish peroxidase conjugated goat anti-rat secondary antibody subsequently according to the manufacturer's protocol. The images of these sections were acquired using Leica microscope.

The analysis data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation of at least triplicate samples. One-way or two-way ANOVA was employed to obtain the significant difference, and a Tukey post-hoc test was followed.

The morphology of CDs was obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1A and B, the prepared CD1/4 were about 2–9 nm with average size of 5.11 ± 1.32 nm. The as-prepared CDs showed relatively high absorption in ultraviolet (UV) light region (Fig. 1C). Compared to CD0, the peaks around 287 nm decreased in CD1/4. The peak around 287 nm could be assigned to the π-π* transition for aromatic sp2 hybridization [25], indicating the decreasing of aromatic sp2 hybridization as the amount of ethylenediamine increased. FTIR spectrum was performed to investigate functional groups on the CDs. Both lysine and CDs have sharp peaks at 2900 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1, which could be assigned to asymmetric- and symmetric- stretching of CH2 respectively (Fig. 1D) [26,27]. Compared to lysine, a new peak around 1510 cm−1 appeared in CD0. This peak could be assigned to CC stretching [28], suggesting formation of sp2 conjugated frameworks in the carbon cores. However, this peak decreased with the increasing of ethylenediamine. This could be attributed to the addition reaction between ethylenediamine and CC bond in carbon cores [29]. Raman spectra were further used to investigate the chemical bonds. Compared to lysine, a new peak around 1475 cm−1 appeared in CD0 (Fig. 1E). However, this peak disappeared in CD1/4 and CD1/3. As this peak could be attributed to CC bonds [30], these results indicated formation of sp2 conjugated frameworks in CD0 and disappearance of sp2-hybridized structure in CD1/4 and CD1/3.

The presence of C, N, and O in CDs and their chemical compositions were further investigated by XPS measurements. The three peaks at ∼284.7, ∼400.9, and ∼531.8 eV could be attributed to characteristic peaks of carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s), respectively (Fig. 2A–C). These results showed that the samples contained the same elements. High resolution C 1s spectra of CDs could be divided into three binding energy subpeaks (Fig. 2D–F), which include 284.1 eV (C=C/C-C), 285.2 eV (CN) and 287.3 eV (C=O) [31,32]. The peak of C=C/C-C increased from 49.01 % in lysine to 58.96 % in CD0 (Table S2), suggesting formation of CC bonds during carbonization. The peak of C=C/C-C decreased from 58.96 % in CD0 to 44.30 % in CD1/3. This indicated the addition reaction between CC and ethylenediamine, which was consistent with the results of FTIR and UV–vis absorption spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dissolution properties of CDs on different metal iron-alginate hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 showed the effect of concentration of CD1/4 on the dissolving rates of Cu2+-alginate hydrogels. The dissolving rate increased with the increasing concentration of CD1/4, but the increasing speed became slow at the concentration of 110 mg/mL. So, CDs aqueous solution at the concentration of 90 mg/mL was chosen in the following study. Fig. 3A and B showed Cu2+-alginate hydrogel dissolved completely after incubation with CD1/4 (90 mg/mL) for 16 min, but other metal ion-alginate hydrogels did not dissolve completely. Fig. 3C showed dissolving rate of metal ion-hydrogels was Cu2+ > Fe2+ > Mn2+ > Zn2+ > Ca2+. This was in accordance with the fluorescence quenching effect of metal ions for CD1/4, which was Cu2+ > Fe2+ > Mn2+ > Zn2+ > Ca2+ (Fig. 3D). CD1/4 in this work showed much stronger complex ability to Cu2+ than other metal ions such as Ca2+. But traditional complexing agent–EDTA has relatively high complex ability to a great number of metal ions including Ca2+, which may cause calcium depletion (hypocalcemia). Static quenching in the presence of metal ions was largely owing to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between CDs and metal ions [31,33]. CDs with nitrogen/oxygen containing groups are considered to be the electron donors, while Cu2+ is regarded as electron acceptors [34]. The electron transfer from nitrogen/oxygen containing groups on the CDs to Cu2+ leads to fluorescence quenching of CDs. Previous works have reported Cu2+ exhibits a higher affinity for nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs than other metal ions [35]. Thus, it could be inferred that higher complexion ability between CDs and metal ions led to higher dissolving rate. Due to the strongest complexation ability and fastest-dissolving rate, Cu2+ and Cu2+-alginate hydrogel were chosen as the research models in the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complexation abilities of CDs toward Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric responses were applied to investigate the complexation between CDs and Cu2+. An immediate color change from light blue (color of Cu2+) to dark purple was occurred, after adding CD1/3, CD1/4 or ethylenediamine (EDA) into Cu2+ solutions (Fig. 4A). As for CD1/7, CD1/15, CD0 and lysine, color changed from light blue to dark blue immediately. Fig. 4B showed the light absorption of 2 μmol Cu2+ mixing with 20 mg CDs. The absorption peaks of CDs blue shifted from 620 nm to 548 nm, as the mass ratio of ethylenediamine to lysine increased from 0 to 1/3. The region between 590 and 620 nm belongs to yellow region, and its complementary color is blue. The region between 548 and 590 belongs to green region, and its complementary color is purple. Thus, these results indicated color changed from blue to purple with the increasing amount of ethylenediamine.

The concentration of Cu2+ also plays important roles during formation of Cu2+-CDs complex. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the light absorption increased (Fig. 4C–F). The increasing of light absorption could be attributed to the higher concentration of Cu2+ and more Cu2+-CDs complexes. However, the light absorption of Cu2+ from 548 nm to 620 nm was quite low (Fig. S2). Thus, formation of more Cu2+-CDs complexes is the major reason for the increasing of light absorption. After reaching a certain maximum value, the light absorption stopped increasing. The slight increasing in the region of >550 nm could be attributed to the elevated Cu2+ concentration. It is also interesting to note that the absorption peak in CD1/4 shifted from 550 to 587 with the elevated Cu2+ concentration, which suggested formation of different Cu2+-CDs complexes.

FTIR spectrum in Fig. S3 also indicated formation of different Cu2+-CDs complexes, when CD1/4 reacted with different concentrations of Cu2+. When 2 μmol Cu2+ was mixed with 20 mg CD1/4, the band at 1649 cm−1 (assigned to -NH2 groups) significantly increased. The varied chemical environment for the NH2 groups indicated that low concentration of Cu2+ preferentially combined with densely clustered ethylenediamine groups. When 7 μmol Cu2+ was mixed with 20 mg CDs, the asymmetric COO− stretching vibration (νasymCOO−) and symmetric COO stretching vibration (νsymCOO−) shifted toward lower wavenumbers (Table S3). The value for Δν (Δν = νasymCOO− − νsymCOO−) of CDs with7 μmol Cu2+ was smaller than that of CDs with 0–2 μmol Cu2+. This indicated Cu2+ also coordinated with COO−, when the concentration of copper ion continues to increase. So, it could be inferred Cu2+ coordinate with ethylenediamine groups and the surrounding lysine groups at high concentrations of Cu2+.

According to previous reports, lysine is coordinated to Cu2+ via the -NH2 and -COO− donor groups in a “glycine-like” manner, in which blue four-coordinate Cu2+ complexes were formed [36,37]. When lysine made up a large proportion of starting materials, the complexation property of CDs was more like to lysine. As the ratio of lysine reduced, the complexation property of CDs was more like to ethylenediamine (Fig. 4G). Since color change to purple is usually assigned to six-coordinate Cu2+ complexes [38], the complex reaction between Cu2+ and CD1/3/CD1/4/EDA could lead to formation of purple six-coordinate species. The previous works also have reported that compounds including ethylenediamine or its derivates are preferably capable of forming five-membered ring complexes, when they interact with metal ions [39]. Five membered rings have been considered as one of the most stable metal complexes [40]. Thus, the above results indicated strong complexation ability of CD1/3 and CD1/4 toward Cu2+.

The surface morphology of the Cu2+-alginate hydrogel was detected by SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the hydrogel showed an interconnected porous structure with homogeneous pores. The stability of the hydrogel was measured by rheological frequency sweep. Fig. S4 showed that storage moduli G' was considerably higher than loss moduli G″ and G′ exhibited a stationary state in the range 0.1–10 rad/s, indicating well-obtained Cu2+-alginate hydrogels. Fig. S5 showed the results of MTT assay. It was found that the cell viability was >95 % for Cu2+-alginate hydrogels before and after dissolution at the concentration of 0.1-10 mg/mL, indicating quite low cytotoxicity. Fig. 5B showed that Cu2+-alginate hydrogels turned into purple solution after incubating with CD1/4, CD1/3 and ethylenediamine for 16 min, which had the same color change in Fig. 4A. These three dissolving agents had faster degradation rates than the other four agents. This could be attributed to higher complexing capacity of the former three.

Fig. 5C showed that the dissolution rate of Cu2+-alginate hydrogel was CD1/3 > CD1/4 > CD1/5 > CD1/15 > lysine > CD0. The dissolving ability of CDs increased with the increasing amount of ethylenediamine, which was in accordance with the complexion ability between Cu2+ and CDs. Compared to lysine, the dissolving rate of CD0 decreased. This may be that some of the lysine participated in formation of carbon cores. But after adding ethylenediamine, the dissolving rate quickly exceeded lysine. Both CD1/3 and CD1/4 could degrade hydrogels within 16 min. However, the cell viability of CD1/3 (10 mg/mL) was significantly lower than that of control group (p < 0.01, Fig. S6). This may be originated from high concentration of ethylenediamine in CD1/3 [41]. As other CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity, CD1/4 was chosen as dissolving agent in the following study. Ability of CD1/4 to capture Cu2+ from hydrogel was also verified by dialysis and FTIR researches. High concentration of NaCl, with the ability to rise the concentration of counter-ions, was added to break the electrostatic interactions between CD1/4 and alginate [23]. The purple degraded solution turned into colorless after dialysis in NaCl solution, while Cu2+-alginate hydrogel had no change (Fig. S7). This indicated the ability of CD1/4 to damage the complexation between Cu2+ and sodium alginate. The FTIR spectra were presented in Fig. 5D. Compared to the sodium alginate, the νasymCOO− and νsymCOO− in copper-alginate complex shifted toward lower wavenumbers. The Δν value of copper-alginate complex was lower than that of sodium alginate (Table S4). These peaks turned back again after removal of Cu2+ by CD1/4. In all, the above results suggested the ability of CD1/4 to capture Cu2+ in Cu2+-alginate hydrogels.

Fig. 6A showed the photographs of burn wounds during the healing process for (1) control group, (2) Cu2+-alginate hydrogel without replacement, (3) Cu2+-alginate hydrogel replaced by CD1/4, respectively. On day 0, the light-yellow exudates from wounds suggested successful establishment of burn wound models. In all three groups, the burn wounds contracted with time (Fig. 6B). The three groups had no significant differences on the wound size after 8 days of treatment. The difference occurred after 12 days of treatment. Compared to hydrogels without replacement and control groups, wound size reduced more in the hydrogels replaced by CD1/4. There was less than one-third of unclosed wound area (22.31 ± 5.3 %) in hydrogel with replacement after 16 days of healing. It took 19 days for control group and hydrogel group to reduce the wound area to 31.67 ± 6.1 %, 27.22 ± 4.6 %, respectively. There were no obvious wounds in the hydrogel with replacement on day 19, indicating the fastest healing rate.

Haemotoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was applied to further investigate wound repair of hydrogel on rats. Fig. 6C displayed the histological results of the wounds on postoperative day 19. The wound in the hydrogels with replacement was almost completely healed. Hair follicles could be found in the tissue, which further confirmed that the wound was almost healed [42]. Collagen deposition was detected by Masson staining. The wound in the hydrogels with replacement showed more densely packed collagen fibers (blue stained) than the other two groups, with P < 0.05 for hydrogels only and P < 0.01 for control groups (Fig. 6D). These results indicated the replacement promoted the reepithelialization and collagen generation of wounds [5,43].

To test whether the wound dressing replacement alleviating hypoxia, immunohistochemistry (IHC) analysis with HIF-1α antibodies (brown cells) was performed. The HIF-1α-positive cell density in the replacement group was significantly lower than the other two groups (Fig. 6E, P < 0.01 for hydrogels only and P < 0.001 for control groups), suggesting that the replaced hydrogels could ameliorate hypoxic conditions. Local inflammation also plays a critical role in the wound healing process. IHC staining of CD68 (brown cells) showed CD68-positive macrophage density in the replacement groups was much lower than the other two groups (Fig. 6F, P < 0.05 for hydrogels only and P < 0.01 for control groups), indicating that the replaced hydrogels could alleviate local inflammatory reactions.

The dissolving hydrogels in this work exhibited attractive functions including hypoxia relief and inflammation decreasing. The decreasing of inflammation may be related to the antibacterial properties of Cu2+, while hypoxia relief could be attributed to better permeability of hydrogels with replacement. The hydrogels absorb the wound exudate, but at the same time the exudate blocks the holes of hydrogels. The replaceable hydrogels could form new hydrogels with porous structure on the wounds, which enhance permeability of hydrogels. As some of the burn wounds are subjected to poor oxygen supply and excessive inflammation, replaceable hydrogel represents an effective treatment method for accelerating burn wound healing without using drugs. Beyond burn wound healing, the developed dissolving hydrogel may be also used to treat diabetic wounds. There are still some limitations of this dissolving hydrogel. For example, the dissolving rate needs to be further accelerated to realize immediate wound dressing replacement. More extensive researches are also needed to dissolve hydrogels based on other metal ions. We will further study these issues in the following study.

A series of CDs using lysine and ethylenediamine as starting materials were prepared to capture metal ions from alginate hydrogels, which would further dissolve the hydrogels for wound dressing replacement. The dissolving rate of CDs for metal ion-alginate hydrogel was Cu2+ > Fe2+ > Mn2+ > Zn2+ > Ca2+, which was in accordance with the complexation ability between metal ions and CDs. The dissolving ability of CDs toward Cu2+-alginate hydrogel increased with the increasing ratio of ethylenediamine groups in CDs. When the mass ratio of ethylenediamine to lysine in CDs was 1/4, the CDs had low cytotoxicity and could form six-coordinate complexes with five-membered rings. The dissolving hydrogels exhibited hypoxia relief and inflammation decreasing. This work highlights the dissolution of Cu2+-alginate hydrogels through complexation reaction between CDs and Cu2+, the effect of starting materials on complexing ability, and their successful application as burn wound dressings.

Qian Li: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xin Shen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Software. Chenguang Liu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Dongming Xing: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020MH324 and ZR2020QH271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.124455.
